1:
wayyeʾĕsōp yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-šibṭê yiśrāʾēl šĕkemāh — 'Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem.' The verb ʾāsap (gather, collect) is used for solemn assemblies; the site Shechem (šĕkem) carries enormous weight. Genesis 12:6 places Abraham's first Canaanite altar at 'the oak of Moreh at Shechem'; Jacob buried his household's foreign gods here (Genesis 35:4); the Samaritan tradition identified it as the central sacred site. Joshua frames the covenant renewal at the place where the land-promise was first given and where idols were first renounced.

wayyiqqāhălû lĕpanê hāʾĕlōhîm — 'they presented themselves before God.' The assembly is a theophanic encounter, not a political rally; standing before hāʾĕlōhîm is the vocabulary of divine court and covenant ceremony.
2:
kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'thus says the LORD God of Israel.' This is the prophetic messenger formula (kōh ʾāmar) — the standard opening of prophetic oracles (Amos 1:3; Jeremiah 2:2; Ezekiel 2:4). Joshua is not speaking in his own name but as YHWH's messenger, introducing the Heilsgeschichte in YHWH's first-person voice. The formula signals that what follows is divinely authoritative speech, not human historical summary.

mēʿôlām ('long ago, from of old') + wayyēšĕbû ʿēber hannāhār ('they lived beyond the River') — the temporal and geographic double-frame: Israel's origins are both ancient and foreign. wayyaʿabdû ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — 'and they served other gods.' YHWH begins his self-presentation by grounding the covenant in a history that starts with Israel's ancestors in idolatry, not in their righteousness. The covenant is entirely God's initiative.
3: wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-ʾăbîkem ʾet-ʾabrāhām mēʿēber hannāhār — 'I took your ancestor Abraham from beyond the River.' The first-person verb-chain begins: wāʾeqqaḥ (I took), wāʾōlēk (I led), wāʾarbeh (I multiplied). YHWH's acts are the exclusive subject; Abraham and the patriarchs are objects, not agents. This syntactic choice is theological: the covenant is founded on divine initiative at every stage, not on human merit or response.
4: waʾettēn lĕyiṣḥāq ʾet-yaʿăqōb wĕʾet-ʿēśāw — 'to Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau.' The verb nātan (give) governs the patriarchal history — God gave the descendants, the land, and eventually the nation. wāʾettēn lĕʿēśāw ʾet-har śēʿîr lāreše't — 'I gave Esau the hill country of Seir as a possession.' The inclusion of Esau's portion acknowledges YHWH's sovereignty over all land distribution, not just Israel's; Edom's land is equally a divine grant. This forestalls any claim that YHWH is merely Israel's tribal deity.
5: wāʾešlaḥ ʾet-mōšeh wĕʾet-ʾahărōn waʾeggōp ʾet-miṣrayim — 'I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt.' The verb nāgap (strike, plague) is used specifically of divine plague-action (Exodus 12:23; Numbers 14:12); it is YHWH who plagues, Moses and Aaron being his instruments. The pairing of sending with striking — 'I sent... and I struck' — maintains the divine agency throughout. The people are brought out, not by their courage or Moses's leadership, but by YHWH's direct action.
6: wāʾôṣîʾ ʾet-ʾăbôtêkem mimmṣrayim wattāboʾû hayyāmmāh — 'I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, and they came to the sea.' The crossing from Egypt to the sea (Reed Sea) is narrated with the same first-person divine action: wāʾôṣîʾ ('I brought out'). The Egyptians pursue with rekeb ûpārāšîm (chariots and cavalry) — exactly the military technology that Israel was forbidden to build (Deuteronomy 17:16; Joshua 11:6); the contrast is made visible: YHWH defeats the chariot-and-cavalry superpower without Israel needing to match it.
7: wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-YHWH wayyāśem maʾăpēl bênêkem ûbên hammiṣrîm — 'they cried to the LORD, and he put darkness between you and the Egyptians.' The ʾōpel (thick darkness, deep gloom) at the sea is mentioned only here and in Exodus 14:20 (the pillar of cloud/fire that came between the camps). Joshua's Heilsgeschichte preserves a detail that could be lost in the standard retelling. wayyābēʾ ʿālāyw ʾet-hayyām wayyĕkassēhû — 'he brought the sea over them and covered them.' YHWH acts as subject; the sea is his instrument.
8: wāʾābîʾ ʾetkhem ʾel-ʾereṣ hāʾĕmōrî — 'I brought you to the land of the Amorites.' The trans-Jordan victories (Sihon, Og) are narrated as YHWH-given: wayyillāḥămû ʾittĕkem wāʾettēn ʾōtām bĕyedkem — 'they fought against you and I gave them into your hand.' The conquest is YHWH's giving, not Israel's fighting — the same formula used throughout Joshua 6-11. wattîrĕšû ʾet-ʾarṣām — 'and you took possession of their land' — only after the divine giving does human possession follow.
9: wayyāqom bālāq ben-ṣippôr melek môʾāb wayyillāḥem bĕyiśrāʾēl — 'Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, rose up and fought against Israel.' The verb nišlaḥ ('sent for') frames Balak's use of Balaam as a mercenary curse-weapon. wayyišlaḥ wayyiqrāʾ lĕbilʿām ben-bĕʿôr lĕqallēl ʾetkhem — 'he sent and called for Balaam son of Beor to curse you.' The narrative covers Num 22-24 in two verses; the speed signals that the Balaam episode is already well-known to the audience — Joshua is invoking shared memory, not narrating events.
10: wĕlōʾ-ʾābîtî lišmōaʿ lĕbilʿām waybārek bārēk ʾetkhem — 'but I refused to listen to Balaam, and he blessed you again and again.' The verb ʾābāh (be willing, consent) used negatively: YHWH refused to be moved by Balak's hired curse. bārēk bārēk is the infinitive absolute construction intensifying the main verb — 'he blessed you repeatedly, thoroughly' — emphatic and iterative. The same grammatical pattern as Genesis 2:17 (mōt tāmût, 'you shall surely die') but here positive: the blessing was emphatic and repeated.
11: wattaʿabrû ʾet-hayyardēn wattābōʾû ʾel-yĕrîḥô wayyillāḥămû bākem baʿălê yĕrîḥô — 'you crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho, and the lords of Jericho fought against you.' The Heilsgeschichte reaches the conquest narrative of the book itself: Jericho, then the coalition of peoples (Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites) — the standard seven-nation list. wāʾettēn ʾōtām bĕyedkem — 'and I gave them into your hand' — the YHWH-war formula that has governed every battle in the book.
12: wāʾešlaḥ lipnêkem ʾet-haṣṣirʿāh — 'I sent the hornet before you.' The ṣirʿāh (hornet, wasp) as a divine weapon appears in Exodus 23:28 and Deuteronomy 7:20; here it is said to have driven out the two Amorite kings, adding a detail not in the battle narratives themselves. Whether the hornet is literal (wasp swarms as divine warfare), metaphorical (referring to Egypt's campaigns that weakened Canaan before Israel arrived), or an angelic divine agent, the point is consistent: lōʾ bĕḥarbekā wĕlōʾ bĕqaštekā — 'not by your sword or bow.' YHWH goes out of his way to deny Israel's weapons the credit.
13: wāʾettēn lākem ʾereṣ ʾašer lōʾ-yāgaʿtā bāh — 'I gave you a land you did not toil for.' This verse quotes Deuteronomy 6:10-11 almost verbatim ('cities you did not build, vineyards and olive groves you did not plant'). The repetition is deliberate: this is not merely history but covenant instruction. The abundance they now enjoy (cities, vineyards, olive orchards) is pure grace — they contributed nothing to its creation. The gift sets the stage for the covenant demand: what will you do with what you did not earn?
14: wĕʿattāh yĕrāʾû ʾet-YHWH wĕʿibdû ʾōtô bĕtāmîm ûbĕʾĕmet — 'now therefore, fear the LORD and serve him in completeness and truth.' The adverbial pair bĕtāmîm ûbĕʾĕmet qualifies the required service: tāmîm means wholeness, integrity, completeness (the same root as millēʾ ʾaḥărê, wholehearted following); ʾĕmet means faithfulness, reliability, truth. The combination demands a service that is both inwardly complete and externally consistent — no divided loyalty, no hidden compromise. wĕhāsîrû ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾašer-ʿābĕdû ʾăbôtêkem — 'put away the gods your ancestors served' — the imperative hāsîr (remove, put away) is strong; this is not a gentle suggestion but a formal covenant demand. Jacob used the same verb at Shechem (Genesis 35:2).
15: ûʾim rāʿ bĕʿênêkem laʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH biḥărû lākem hayyôm ʾet-mi taʿăbōdûn — 'if it seems evil to you to serve the LORD, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve.' The verb bāḥar (choose, elect) places the burden of decision on Israel; YHWH does not coerce. Hayyôm ('today') is the covenant urgency word — Deuteronomy's characteristic call to immediate decision (Deuteronomy 4:40; Joshua 5:1; Joshua 26:16). wĕʾānōkî ûbêtî naʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH — 'but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.' Joshua's ʾānōkî (emphatic 'I') stands against the hypothetical choice; his household's commitment is personal and public, declared before witnesses.
16: ḥālîlāh lānû mēʿăzōb ʾet-YHWH laʿăbōd ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — 'far be it from us to forsake the LORD to serve other gods.' Ḥālîlāh is an oath-like repudiation formula ('God forbid! Far be it!'), used at moments of extreme horror (Genesis 18:25; Joshua 44:7; 1 Samuel 14:45; 2 Samuel 23:17). The strength of the formula signals that the people understand the gravity of what they are being asked; they are not responding perfunctorily.
17: Israel's own Heilsgeschichte recital: they repeat back the covenant history YHWH narrated in Joshua 24:2-13, now in their own words. This dialogical structure (God narrates → people narrate back) mirrors ancient covenant ceremony where both parties confess the history that grounds the treaty. ki YHWH ʾĕlōhênû hûʾ hammaʿăleh ʾōtānû — 'for the LORD our God, he is the one who brought us up.' The hûʾ ('he is') is emphatic identification — YHWH, not any other power, is the agent of their history.
18: waygāreš YHWH ʾet-kol-hāʿammîm — 'the LORD drove out all the peoples before us.' The verb gāraš (drive out, expel) is used of expelling parties from covenant — the same word used when God expelled Adam from Eden (Genesis 3:24). Israel's confession attributes the conquest entirely to YHWH: gam-ʾănaḥnû naʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH kî hûʾ ʾĕlōhênû — 'we also will serve the LORD, for he is our God.' Their commitment is grounded in his identity and acts, not in their ability to maintain it.
19:
wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-hāʿām lōʾ tûkĕlûn laʿăbōd ʾet-YHWH — 'Joshua said to the people: you are not able to serve the LORD.' The shock of this statement — coming immediately after the people's enthusiastic confession — is intentional. Joshua is not discouraging commitment but exposing its inadequacy: their confidence in themselves is itself the problem.

kî-ʾĕlōhîm qĕdōšîm hûʾ ʾēl-qannōʾ hûʾ — 'for he is a holy God; he is a jealous God.' Note the grammatical anomaly: ʾĕlōhîm is grammatically plural ('Gods') but the adjective qĕdōšîm is also plural, agreeing with the noun form, while hûʾ ('he') is singular, identifying YHWH as a single person. This is the intensive/majestic plural (Hebrew often uses plural forms to convey transcendent greatness). Qannōʾ (jealous) is the technical covenant term — YHWH's exclusive-loyalty demand (Exodus 20:5; Joshua 34:14); breaking covenant with him is not merely an error but a personal offense against a party who will not share his rights.

lōʾ-yiśśāʾ lĕpišʿăkem wĕlĕḥaṭṭōʾtêkem — 'he will not forgive your transgressions and sins.' The word nāśāʾ (bear, lift, carry) is the standard term for God's forgiveness — lifting and carrying away sin (Genesis 50:17; Psalms 32:5; Micah 7:18). YHWH's refusal to bear pešaʿ (rebellion/transgression, the deliberate covenant-breaking term) or ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin, missing the mark) is presented as a consequence of his holiness and jealousy — his very character.
20: kî taʿăzebû ʾet-YHWH wĕʿăbadtem ʾĕlōhê nēkār — 'if you forsake the LORD and serve foreign gods.' The verb ʿāzab (forsake, abandon) is the covenant-rupture term — the same word used when men leave father and mother (Genesis 2:24), but here applied to the ultimate covenantal relationship. wĕšāb wĕhērāʿ lākem — 'he will turn and harm you.' The šûb ('turn') of YHWH toward the covenant-breaker is the reversal of his protective turning toward Israel; the same divine responsiveness that brought blessing now brings judgment.
21: wayyōʾmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ lōʾ kî ʾet-YHWH naʿăbōd — 'the people said to Joshua: No! We will serve the LORD.' The emphatic lōʾ kî ('No! Rather...') is a strong counter-assertion. Having heard Joshua's stark warning about their inability (Joshua 24:19), the people double down rather than retreat — which sets up the covenant's formal ratification in Joshua 24:22-27. Their persistence after the warning shows either genuine faith or dangerous self-confidence; the ambiguity is part of the human covenant condition.
22: wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-hāʿām ʿēdîm ʾattem bākem — 'Joshua said to the people: you are witnesses against yourselves.' The plural ʿēdîm (witnesses) is the legal vocabulary of Israelite court procedure; the covenant assembly is simultaneously a juridical assembly. Bākem — 'against yourselves' — the force of the preposition: their own declaration becomes the evidence against them if they defect. The people's response (ʿēdîm, 'witnesses') accepts the self-incriminating oath. This is one of the oldest attested covenant-ratification formulas in the OT.
23: wĕʿattāh hāsîrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēkār ʾašer bĕqirbĕkem — 'now therefore put away the foreign gods that are among you.' The command hāsîrû (put away, remove) is the same as Joshua 24:14, now repeated with the revealing addition: ʾašer bĕqirbĕkem — 'which are among you.' The gods were not left behind in Egypt or Mesopotamia; they are present within the covenant community, hidden (bĕqereb = in the midst, in the interior). The covenant demand is not about past idolatry but current syncretism. wĕhaṭṭû ʾet-lĕbabkem ʾel-YHWH — 'incline your hearts to the LORD' — the verb nāṭāh (stretch, extend, incline) asks for the direction of the will, not merely an external act.
24: wayyōʾmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû naʿăbōd ûbĕqōlô nišmāʿ — 'the people said to Joshua: the LORD our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey.' The double commitment pairs ʿābad (serve, worship) with šāmaʿ bĕqôl (obey, lit. 'hear in the voice'). Both terms carry covenant weight: ʿābad is the vocabulary of vassal service to a suzerain; šāmaʿ bĕqôl is the Deuteronomic obedience formula (Deuteronomy 13:4; 1 Samuel 15:22). The people align themselves as covenant vassals before the divine suzerain.
25: wayyikrōt yĕhôšuaʿ bĕrît lāʿām bayyôm hahûʾ wayyāśem lô ḥōq ûmišpāṭ biškem — 'Joshua cut a covenant with the people that day, and set for them statute and ordinance at Shechem.' Three legal terms accumulate: bĕrît (covenant, the treaty-relationship itself), ḥōq (statute, fixed rule, carved-in obligation), mišpāṭ (ordinance, judgment, binding decision). The verb kārat bĕrît ('cut a covenant') is the standard idiom, preserving the ancient practice of cutting animals to ratify treaties (Genesis 15:10,17). The location Shechem is the same site as Jacob's putting-away of gods (Genesis 35:4) — every Shechem scene in Genesis-Joshua involves the removal of foreign gods and the reassertion of covenant loyalty to YHWH.
26:
wayyiktōb yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh bĕsēper tôrat ʾĕlōhîm — 'Joshua wrote these things in the Book of the Law of God.' This is one of only a few OT passages that explicitly describes the extension of the written Torah — the covenant speech becomes part of the authoritative scroll. Sēper tôrat ʾĕlōhîm is likely the Mosaic Torah or an updated copy; Joshua's additions are authoritative because he speaks under the prophetic messenger formula (Joshua 24:2) and by divine command.

wayyiqqaḥ ʾeben gĕdôlāh wayyĕqîmehā šām taḥat hāʾallāh ʾašer bĕmiqdaš YHWH — 'he took a large stone and set it up there under the oak at the sanctuary of the LORD.' The oak (ʾallāh) at the sanctuary of YHWH connects to the oak of Moreh at Shechem (Genesis 12:6) — the tree where Israel's story in the land began is now the witness-stone's location. The covenant is sealed at the place where the promise was first given.
27: wayyōʾmer yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-kol-hāʿām hinnēh hāʾeben hazzōʾt tihyeh-bānû lĕʿēdāh — 'this stone shall be a witness against us.' The stone is assigned legal standing as a ʿēd (witness, legal testimony-giver). This is not animism — the stone does not have a soul — but the employment of covenant-witness language for an enduring physical object. The rationale: kî-hîʾ šāmĕʿāh ʾēt kol-ʾimrê YHWH ʾašer dibber ʿimmānû — 'for it has heard all the words of the LORD that he spoke to us.' Presence at the covenant assembly constitutes legal knowledge; the stone's physical presence as witness is functionally equivalent to a human witness who heard the oath. The reasoning will reappear in Luke 19:40 — 'the stones would cry out.'
28: wayyĕšallaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-hāʿām ʾîš lĕnaḥălātô — 'Joshua dismissed the people, each to his inheritance.' The verb šillaḥ (send away, dismiss) closes the assembly formally; the naḥălāh (inheritance) is the destination — they go to what YHWH has given, armed with the renewed covenant obligations. The chapter's governing word (naḥălāh appears throughout Joshua 13-22) provides the farewell note: the land-gift and the covenant-obligation are inseparable.
29: wayĕhî ʾaḥărê haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh wayyāmot yĕhôšuaʿ bin-nûn ʿebed YHWH — 'after these things Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died.' The title ʿebed YHWH ('servant of the LORD') was applied to Moses throughout the book (Joshua 1:1,2,7,13; Joshua 8:31,33; Joshua 9:24; Joshua 11:12,15; Joshua 12:6; Joshua 13:8; Joshua 14:7; Joshua 18:7; Joshua 22:2-5); here, for the first time, it is applied to Joshua. The death report formally elevates Joshua to Mosaic status — he died as what Moses was: YHWH's servant. Ben-meʾāh wĕʿeśer šānîm — 110 years, the ideal Egyptian lifespan (Genesis 50:26; Joseph also died at 110); the number signals a life of full completion.
30: wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô bigbûl naḥălātô bĕtimnāt-seraḥ — 'they buried him in the territory of his inheritance at Timnath-serah.' Timnath-serah is the inheritance Caleb-parallel: Joshua received his own territory after all others had been settled (Joshua 19:49-50), as we noted there. His burial in his own allotment anchors his identity with the land-promise: he dies where he was given, in the hill country of Ephraim, miṣṣāpôn lĕhar-gāʿaš — 'north of Mount Gaash.' The geographic specificity marks the site as a real place that could be visited — the claim is historical, not merely symbolic.
31: wayyaʿăbōd yiśrāʾēl ʾet-YHWH kōl yĕmê yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkōl yĕmê hazĕqēnîm ʾašer hĕʾĕrîkû yāmîm ʾaḥărê yĕhôšuaʿ — 'Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua and all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua.' This is the book's theological assessment of the covenant's durability: it held through one generation of leadership. The implicit warning: what happens when that generation passes? Judges 2:10-12 provides the answer — 'another generation arose who did not know the LORD.' The covenant's success was personal and generational, not automatic or permanent.
32: wĕʾet-ʿaṣmôt yôsēp ʾašer-hĕʿělû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl mimmṣrayim qābĕrû biškem — 'the bones of Joseph, which the Israelites had brought from Egypt, they buried at Shechem.' Genesis 50:25 records Joseph's oath: pāqōd yipqōd ʾĕlōhîm ʾetkhem — 'God will surely visit you' — and his command to carry his bones. Exodus 13:19 records Moses taking the bones at the exodus. The burial at Shechem is the completion of a four-generation promise-chain: Joseph's faith that the God who promised Canaan would deliver it (Hebrews 11:22 calls this an act of faith), carried through the wilderness, fulfilled here. bĕḥelqat haśśādeh ʾašer qānāh yaʿăqōb mēʾēt bĕnê-ḥămôr — in the plot Jacob bought from Hamor's sons (Genesis 33:19) — the land transaction from Genesis becomes the burial site in Joshua; purchased land and promised land converge.
33: wĕʾelʿāzār ben-ʾahărōn mēt wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô bigbĕʿat pînĕḥās bĕnô — 'Eleazar son of Aaron died and was buried at Gibeah of his son Phinehas.' The book closes with three burials: Joshua (the military-covenant leader), Joseph (the patriarch whose faith spanned the whole story), and Eleazar (the priestly mediator of the allotments, Joshua 14:1; Joshua 17:4; Joshua 19:51; Joshua 21:1). The triple burial closes the three streams of Israel's leadership — prophetic-military (Joshua), patriarchal-faith (Joseph), and priestly (Eleazar) — each now in the ground of the promised land. The priestly line continues through Phinehas (also named in Psalms 106:30 for his covenant zeal); the story does not end, but the founding generation is laid to rest in the land YHWH gave them.
